ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for mirchi pakoda; mapped to broader fritter class. A more specific term for stuffed chilli fritter does not exist in FoodOn as of April 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fritters and similar deep-fried battered products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for mirchi pakoda or stuffed chilli fritter; A0CKP (fritters and similar deep-fried battered products) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for mirchi pakoda or stuffed chilli fritter as of April 2026.

allergens: gluten (gram flour / besan contains trace gluten risk; cross-contamination possible)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mirchi pakoda consists of 2 large stuffed chilli fritters, weighing approximately 120 g in total, as commonly served as a starter or snack portion in Indian restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Pakora (besan-battered deep-fried snack) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, composite estimate for stuffed chilli pakoda based on component analysis: large green chilli, potato filling, gram flour batter, deep-fried
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for mirchi pakoda as a composite dish. Values are derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) component data: besan (gram flour) per 100 g, boiled potato per 100 g, green chilli per 100 g, and standard deep-frying oil absorption factor (~15–18% fat uptake for battered fritters). The composite per-100 g estimate reflects approximately 40% gram flour batter (post-fry), 35% potato filling, and 25% green chilli by cooked weight, consistent with published recipe analyses for mirchi pakoda. Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries for similar battered deep-fried vegetable fritters (SR Legacy #18928 and comparable items).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of besan (chickpea flour) and potato-based deep-fried snacks — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for chickpea flour products and potato-based fried snacks
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mirchi pakoda in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on: (1) besan (gram flour) GI ~28–35 (low; University of Sydney GI Database, chickpea flour entries); (2) boiled potato GI ~78 (high; University of Sydney GI Database); (3) deep-frying fat content slows gastric emptying and blunts glycemic response; (4) green chilli fibre and capsaicin further moderate postprandial glucose. Weighted composite estimate for the dish (40% besan batter, 35% potato filling, 25% green chilli by cooked weight) yields an estimated GI of approximately 60–68; midpoint 65 used. Glycemic load per 120 g serving = (65 × 29.8 × 1.20) / 100 = 23.2 g. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g) and deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit mirchi pakoda to occasional consumption, as the sodium content from added salt and spice blends (~310 mg/100 g) combined with a typical 120 g serving delivers approximately 372 mg sodium, contributing meaningfully toward the 1500–2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~65) and moderate glycemic load (~23 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume mirchi pakoda in small portions and infrequently, as the medium GI (~65) and glycemic load of approximately 23 per standard serving can produce a moderate postprandial glucose rise; pairing with a low-GI food or protein source may help blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat from deep-frying (~14.5 g/100 g) and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat mirchi pakoda as an occasional food, given its elevated total fat content (~14.5 g/100 g) from deep-frying in vegetable oil and its moderate sodium load; the saturated fat content is relatively low (~1.8 g/100 g), which is a partial mitigating factor, but overall energy density and frying oil intake remain concerns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mirchi Pakoda (also called Mirchi Pakora or Mirchi Bajji) is a popular Indian deep-fried snack made from large green chillies that are slit open, stuffed with a spiced mashed potato mixture, dipped in a thick gram flour (besan) batter seasoned with salt, turmeric, red chilli powder, and cumin, and then deep-fried in hot oil until golden and crisp. The result is a crunchy, savoury fritter with a mildly spicy to moderately spicy interior depending on the variety of chilli used and the spice level of the potato filling. The outer batter is crisp and slightly nutty from the besan, while the inside offers a soft, warmly spiced potato filling contrasted by the heat of the green chilli. Mirchi pakoda is a staple street food and restaurant starter across North India, particularly in Rajasthan, Punjab, and Uttar Pradesh, and is widely served in Indian restaurants globally including in Singapore. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 265 kcal, 6.2 g protein, 14.5 g fat (predominantly from frying oil), and 29.8 g carbohydrate. The gram flour batter contributes plant protein and folate, while the green chilli provides vitamin C (~28 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (~3.2 g/100 g). The estimated glycemic index is approximately 65 (medium), and a standard 120 g serving carries a glycemic load of approximately 23. Clinically, mirchi pakoda is a moderate-concern food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its medium GI, moderate sodium content, and elevated fat from deep-frying. It is vegetarian, vegan, halal, and dairy-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary preferences, though it is not gluten-free due to gram flour.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mirchi pakoda?
Mirchi pakora vs mirchi bajji difference

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for mirchi pakoda as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN component data for besan, potato, and green chilli with a standard deep-frying fat absorption factor, cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy analogues. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for chickpea flour products and potato-based fried snacks. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-based derivation uses well-established approved sources and the dish composition is well-documented.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-derived estimates based on ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for besan, boiled potato, and green chilli, combined with a standard deep-frying oil absorption factor of approximately 15–18%. No single approved-source entry for mirchi pakoda as a composite dish was found. Values should be replaced with a direct laboratory analysis or a validated composite entry if one becomes available in ICMR-NIN or HPB databases. 2. GI value of 65 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for mirchi pakoda is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 3. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as selenium content for this dish is not reported in ICMR-NIN component data or USDA SR Legacy entries used for cross-check. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: mirchi pakoda is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally zero. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero: this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero: no animal-derived or fortified ingredients are present; vitamin D is naturally zero in this dish. 7. The input food_name 'Mirchi Pakoda (medium spicy)' has been normalised to canonical form 'Mirchi Pakoda'; the spice-level qualifier is retained in aliases. 8. Serving size of 120 g (2 pieces) is an estimate based on typical restaurant portion sizes for mirchi pakoda in Indian restaurants in Singapore; actual serving weights may vary.
